coredata.dc:title: Deciphering size-induced influence of carbon dots on mechanical performance of cement composites
coredata.dc:creator: He, Haijie
Shuang, E.
Lu, Dong
Hu, Yin
Yan, Changwang
Shan, Huafeng
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a newfangled and eco-friendly nanomaterial exhibit a promising engineering application in strengthening mechanical properties of cement matrices. However, the modification effectiveness of CDs on mechanical performance lacks enough investigation. More seriously, the influence of their sizes is still a research gap, enormously hindering their further application in cement-based materials. Herein, the size-induced effect of CDs on mechanical properties of cement mortar is systematically disclosed for the first time. Specifically, three kinds of CDs with diverse sizes are deliberately synthesized via calcinating the cheap and nontoxic raw material (i.e., citric acid). The relationship between the mechanical performance and the sizes of CDs is determined through the compressive strength test. More importantly, the mechanism of their size-induced effect on mechanical properties is comprehensively studied based on the analyses of phase composition and pore structure. The consequences illustrate that the compressive strength of mortar modified by CDs with average sizes of 12.53, 5.86, and 1.81 nm increases by 6.6%, 15.7% and 9.4% separately, compared to the blank group after 7-d curing. With the sizes of CDs reducing ranging from 12.5 to 1.81 nm, the compressive strength of CDs-modified mortar firstly increases and then decreases. The related mechanism is attributed to the combined effect of CDs-induced nucleation and air entrainment. As the sizes of CDs decrease, the intensities of both the nucleation effect and air entrainment effect gradually strengthen, thereby accelerating the cement hydration to generate more C-S-H and introducing the harmful pores. This work fills the research gap of the effect caused by CDs sizes on mechanical properties for cement-based materials. This would guide preparation of CDs with suitable sizes toward increasing mechanical properties of cement matrices, thus facilitating the development of high-performance concrete by CDs-based nanomodification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mechanical performance
Cement
Carbon dots
Size-induced effect
Mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance and phase composition measuring of mortar specimens

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), Project of Taizhou Science and Technology Bureau (23sfa03), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and sizes distributions of different CDs: (a, d) L-CDs; (b, e) M-CDs; (c, f) S-CDs.
AFM images and topographical heights along the white line in AFM images of diverse CDs: (a, d) L-CDs; (b, e) M-CDs; (c, f) S-CDs.

Surface groups, chemical composition, and structural features of as-prepared CDs: (a) FT-IR curves; (b) Full range XPS spectra; (c) Raman spectra; (d-f) C1s spectra of L-CDs, M-CDs, and S-CDs; (g-i) O1s spectra of L-CDs, M-CDs, and S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis (with photographs taken under daylight and a 365 nm UV light after 3-d standing, inset) and (b) maximum PL spectra of diverse CDs-incorporating simulated concrete pore solutions before and after 3-d standing.
(a) Compressive strength of various cement mortar; (b) Increase rates of compressive strength than that of C-M group at diverse curing times.
(a) XRD patterns of diverse mortar after 28-d curing; (b) Phase composition calculated by Rietveld method. (CH: Ca(OH)2; Cc: CaCO3; Si: SiO2; Zn: ZnO).
DTG of all specimens after 28-d curing.
Results of MIP: (a) Cumulative intrusion-pore diameter curve; (b) The pore volume distribution of various specimens.
Schematic of the combined effect of nucleation and air-entrainment on phase composition and pore structure.

head.ce:title: Deciphering size-induced influence of carbon dots on mechanical performance of cement composites

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a newfangled and eco-friendly nanomaterial exhibit a promising engineering application in strengthening mechanical properties of cement matrices. However, the modification effectiveness of CDs on mechanical performance lacks enough investigation. More seriously, the influence of their sizes is still a research gap, enormously hindering their further application in cement-based materials. Herein, the size-induced effect of CDs on mechanical properties of cement mortar is systematically disclosed for the first time. Specifically, three kinds of CDs with diverse sizes are deliberately synthesized via calcinating the cheap and nontoxic raw material (i.e., citric acid). The relationship between the mechanical performance and the sizes of CDs is determined through the compressive strength test. More importantly, the mechanism of their size-induced effect on mechanical properties is comprehensively studied based on the analyses of phase composition and pore structure. The consequences illustrate that the compressive strength of mortar modified by CDs with average sizes of 12.53, 5.86, and 1.81 nm increases by 6.6%, 15.7% and 9.4% separately, compared to the blank group after 7-d curing. With the sizes of CDs reducing ranging from 12.5 to 1.81 nm, the compressive strength of CDs-modified mortar firstly increases and then decreases. The related mechanism is attributed to the combined effect of CDs-induced nucleation and air entrainment. As the sizes of CDs decrease, the intensities of both the nucleation effect and air entrainment effect gradually strengthen, thereby accelerating the cement hydration to generate more C-S-H and introducing the harmful pores. This work fills the research gap of the effect caused by CDs sizes on mechanical properties for cement-based materials. This would guide preparation of CDs with suitable sizes toward increasing mechanical properties of cement matrices, thus facilitating the development of high-performance concrete by CDs-based nanomodification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cement-based materials, as the most extensively used building materials in the world, play an irreplaceable role in social and economic development [1,2]. The requirements of concrete mechanical properties gradually increase with the acceleration of urbanization. Utilizing nanomaterials for nanomodification of cement matrices is regarded as the optimal approach to satisfy the requirement. Currently, nanomaterials such as nano SiO2 [3], nano TiO2 [4], graphene oxide (GO) [5], and layered double hydroxides (LDH) [6] have been employed to enhance the mechanical property of cement composites. The common enhancement mechanisms of nanomaterials are as follows [7–9]: 1) accelerating cement hydration by providing nucleation sites to generate more hydration production; 2) immobilizing free water in cement-based materials; 3) enhancing the particle packing with cement matrix and diminishing the porosity. However, owing to their high specific surface area and surface energy, nanomaterials above suffer from the severe aggregation in cement matrices, resulting in the formation of weak zones. This offsets their positive effect, and even diminishes the mechanical property of cement-based materials [10]. For another thing, nanomaterials above are also susceptible to the high cost. These two intractable issues significantly constrain the application of nanomaterials in mechanical enhancement of cement composites.

Exhilaratingly, carbon dots (CDs), as a novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, exhibit not only excellent dispersity and low cost, but also outstanding optical properties, attractive catalytic behavior, and environmental friendliness. By virtue of these distinguished characteristics, CDs have been widely applied to biomedicine [11], catalysis [12], sensing [13], etc. Recently, CDs have attracted considerable attention in the field of cement-based materials. For instance, our previous work demonstrated that CDs possessed a high dispersity in the simulated concrete pore solution, and could enormously enhance the chloride binding performance of cement pastes [14]. Apart from chloride binding, CDs have also demonstrated positive influence on mechanical behavior of cement matrices. Specifically, Qu et al. firstly utilized CDs with a dosage of 0.08 wt% (by the weight of cement) to enhance the compressive strength of 28-d curing concrete by 16.8% [15]. Subsequently, Li’s group reported that CDs could confer higher density, larger viscosity, and greater stability on foams of foam concrete, thus increasing the mechanical properties of foamed concrete [16]. To sum up, CDs possess enormous potential to improve the properties of cement composites. Nevertheless, up to now, to the best of our knowledge, there are only two reported studies about the enhancement of mechanical behaviors for cement matrices via incorporating CDs. Thus, the studies of CDs as a novel carbon-based nanomaterial in the field of cement composites remain in an infancy stage. This is insufficient to advance the application of CDs in cement-based materials, and a considerable amount of in-depth research is still urgently required.

The previous investigations evidence that the size of CDs is closely correlated with their improvement effectiveness. For example, He et al. reported that CDs with smaller sizes could more easily enter into the frictional interfaces, thereby improving the tribological performance better [17]. Both Zhao’s group and Chen’s group disclosed that the corrosion inhibiting behaviors of CDs gradually enhanced as the sizes of CDs reduced [18,19]. At the same time, the size of nanomaterials is one of the most crucial parameters influencing their modification effect on mechanical actions of cement-based materials. For example, Yu’ group illustrated that LDH with smaller size exhibited the more efficient enhancement of the compressive and the flexural strength of cement [20]. And Sun’ group evidenced that the 5-nm nano-TiO2 resulted in a higher compressive strength of cement paste than 25-nm nano-TiO2 in each curing period [21]. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer that the sizes of CDs have a significant effect on the mechanical enhancement of cement composites. However, the relative research is still a gap. Hence, it is highly urgent to synthesize different CDs containing various sizes, and subsequently investigate the relationship between their modified effect on mechanical performance of cement composites and their sizes. This will conduce to further enhancing the mechanical properties of cement composite by preparing more suitable sizes of CDs.

Considering the fact above, this work successfully synthesizes three types of CDs with average sizes of 12.53, 5.86, and 1.81 nm, comprehensively investigates their influence on mechanical enhancement of mortar, and circumstantiates the underlying mechanism. Specifically, CDs with various sizes are controllably synthesized through altering the calcination time of the raw material (i.e., citric acid). The CDs size-induced influence on mechanical properties of mortar is determined by compressive strength test. And the related mechanism is reasonably proposed through analyses of phase composition (confirmed by XRD and DTG) and pore structure (examined by MIP). This work systematically investigates the relationship between the sizes of CDs and their enhancement effect on mechanical properties of mortar. This would contribute to not only advancing the application of CDs in the field of building materials, but also further promoting the development of high-performance concrete through nano modification.

The citric acid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was utilized throughout the experiment. The cement in this study was Portland cement (PC) PI 52.5 R. The specifical properties and compositions of PC were listed in Table 1 and Table 2. The standard sand conformed to GB/T 17671.

Various-sized CDs were synthesized through pyrolysis treatment, utilizing citric acid as the exclusive precursor. Specifically, 15 g citric acid was added into individual alumina crucible, and subsequently calcinated for 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 h separately at a temperature of 200 °C. After cooling to ambient conditions, the as-obtained products were dissolved in DI water. Then, the mixture was neutralized with NaOH, and subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 10 000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane, followed by dialysis to eliminate insoluble products, sodium salt. Ultimately, the various-sized CDs were prepared through rotary evaporation and free-drying. According to the sizes of CDs, the as-obtained CDs specimens were named as L-CDs, M-CDs, and S-CDs corresponding to the calcination time of 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 h respectively.

To investigate the CDs-induced effect on mechanical properties of cement mortar, four kinds of mortar specimens without/with various CDs were prepared and named as C-M, L-CDs-M, M-CD-M, and S-CDs-M. All the mortar specimens shared a common CDs dosage (0.1 wt% by the weight of cement) and water to cement ratio (w/c=0.42). The specifical mixture proportion of all mortar specimens is shown in Table 3. The detailed preparation process of mortar was as follows. Firstly, the CDs with different sizes were dissolved in DI water irrespectively. Then, the CDs solution was added into cement and sand, and the mixture was stirred through a mixer with a low speed for 120 s and a high speed for 90 s subsequently. Secondly, the as-prepared cement mortar was poured into a 40 mm × 40 mm× 40 mm mold, and protected by a plastic film to reduce moisture vaporization. The mortar specimens were demolded after 1-d curing, and transferred to a curing chamber maintained at 20 °C with a relative humidity exceeding 95% for 28-d curing.

The morphologies of the CDs with different sizes were conducted with FEI Talos F200S transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Brucker MFP-3D infinity atomic force microscopy (AFM). Thermo scientific DXR Raman spectra were utilized to record the structure information of all the CDs. The optical property was characterized using Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and Hitachi F7000 photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies. Furthermore, the functional groups and element composition of various CDs were measured via Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectra in the range of 600–3600 cm−1 and Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and phase composition measuring of mortar specimens
ce:section.ce:para: The compressive performance of CDs-modified mortar at 7-, 14-, and 28-d curing ages was measured based on the Chinese national standard GB/T 17671–2021. To minimize errors, each type of mortar was measured six times. To disclose the mechanism of size-induced effect of CDs on mechanical properties of mortar, XRD and DTG were employed to analyze the phase composition. The prepared process of powder specimens for phase composition tests was as follows. The mortar specimens underwent a 28-d curing were immersed in anhydrous ethanol for 3 days to stop the hydration. Then, the mortar specimens were vacuum-dried at 60 °C until their weight stabilized. After drying, the mortar was ground into powder with particle sizes smaller than 75 μm. XRD was tested through a D8 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation source manufactured by Brucker. It was operated at 40 kV voltage and 40 mA current with a scanning speed of 6°/min. DTG analysis was performed in a flowing N2 atmosphere over a temperature range from 20 to 1000 °C, employing a heating rate of 10 °C/min. In addition, mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) was adopted to assess the pore structure of cement mortar via an AutoPore IV 9500 instrument. The MIP samples, taken from the mortar cores and smaller than 1 cm×1 cm×1 cm while weighing around 2–3 g, were immersed in absolute ethanol for 24 h to terminate hydration and then vacuum-dried at 60 °C until they reached a constant mass.

In this work, CDs with different sizes are controllably synthesized through simply changing the calcinating time of citric acid. TEM and AFM images are executed to assess the sizes distribution of CDs. As shown in TEM images ( Fig. 1 a-c), it is obvious that the sizes of these as-obtained CDs gradually decrease with the prolongation of the calcination time. In addition, all the CDs display quasi-spherical shapes, and share the high dispersity without discernible aggregation. As is well known, the aggregation of nanomaterials tends to compromise their positive effect on modifying the performance of cement-based materials. Thus, the high dispersity endows CDs with a promising potential for application in the cement system. As shown in Fig. 1 d-f, the sizes distribution of various CDs is confirmed via randomly measuring more than 100 particles. Specifically, L-CDs synthesized by 0.5 h calcination own a size distribution from 8.2 to 16.2 nm and an approximate average size of 12.53 nm. When the calcination time reaches 1.0 h, M-CDs possess a size distribution ranging from 2.0 to 10.0 nm with an average size of 5.86 nm. Compared to that of L-CDs, the average size of M-CDs decreases by 53.2%. With the calcination time further extending to 2.0 h, S-CDs with the smallest average size of 1.81 nm present a size distribution between 1.1 and 2.5 nm.

AFM test is executed to further characterize the dispersity and topographical height distributions of as-prepared CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a-c, three kinds of CDs exhibit significantly different sizes, and hold the high dispersity consistent with the observation of TEM. According to the results in Fig. 2 d-f, L-CDs present the highest topographical height ranges from 5.3 to 5.7 nm. With the calcination time prolonging to 1.0 h, the height distribution of M-CDs is within the range of 2.2–2.7 nm. As the calcination time further extends to 2.0 h, S-CDs possess the smallest topographical height between 1.7 and 2.0 nm. In summary, both TEM and AFM tests clearly manifest that three kinds of CDs with various sizes (12.53, 5.86, and 1.81 nm) are successfully prepared. And with the calcination time increasing, the sizes of CDs gradually decrease. Moreover, the as-prepared CDs exhibit excellent dispersity in the aqueous solution.

FTIR, XPS and Raman tests are employed to characterize the functional groups, chemical composition, and structural features of CDs. The FTIR spectra, as depicted in Fig. 3 a, reveal that the three types of CDs display similar absorption peaks. This indicates that the calcination time fails to significantly alter the variety of surface groups. In particular, the peak in the range of 1500–1650 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CC bonds [22]. This unequivocally evidences the presence of sp2 C structure in these CDs. Additionally, the distinct peaks at 1275, 1377, and 3410 cm−1 are associated with the vibrations of C−OH, COO − , and −OH respectively [23,24]. These oxygen-containing groups not only confer high dispersity on CDs [25], but also are beneficial to advancing the cement hydration [26]. As delineated in full range XPS spectra (Fig. 3 b), all the synthesized CDs are composed of the same elements: C, O, and Na. Notably, the Na element originates from the residual NaOH utilized in the neutralization, rather than an intrinsic component of CDs. The high-resolution C1s (Fig. 3 d-f) and O1s (Fig. 3 g-i) spectra are employed to specifically character surface groups of the as-obtained CDs. In the high-resolution C1s spectra, three peaks correspond to CC/C–C, C–O and CO bonds [27]. The C–C/CC bonds constitute the major component with a proportion exceeding 62%, further implying that these CDs are primarily composed of a large number of sp2 C structures. In high-resolution O1s spectra, three distinct peaks are related to CO, C–O and Na KLL, respectively [28]. In addition, the proportions of CC/C–C, CO, and C–O in various CDs are approximately 60%-70%, 24%-32%, and 4%-6% respectively. This suggests that calcination time remarkably affects the sizes of CDs, while exerts a marginal impact on their chemical composition and surface groups. Furthermore, Raman spectra are implemented to investigate the structure information of CDs (Fig. 3 c). All the CDs exhibit two distinct peaks: D band at 1365 cm−1 and G band at 1569 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered sp3 defects and sp2 C, separately. The intensity ratios of these two characteristic bands (I D/I G) from L-CDs, M-CDs, and S-CDs are 0.87, 0.93, and 0.96 separately, indicating that the as-prepared CDs possess favorable crystallinity.

In summary, three CDs with various sizes are successfully synthesized through controllably altering the calcination time of citric acid. These as-prepared CDs exhibit significant variation in sizes, but possess similar surface groups, chemical composition, and crystallinity. This establishes a crucial foundation for investigating the size-induced effect on the mechanical performance of cement-based materials.

Dispersity stability of nanomaterials in cement-based materials is widely recognized as an essential precondition for fully exerting their positive effects [29]. The spectra of UV–vis and PL are adopted to illustrate the dispersion stability of CDs. The optical photographs inserted in Fig. 4 a present that there is no any visible precipitation in all the CDs-including simulated concrete pore solution after 3-d standing. And all the CDs-containing simulated pore solution still emit light blue fluorescence under the exposure of 365 nm UV light. This qualitatively unveils the outstanding dispersity stability of CDs in the stimulated pore solution, since the aggregation of CDs tends to weak, and even vanish fluorescence (namely self-quenching effect) [30]. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the intensities of UV–vis absorption peaks from the CDs-containing simulated pore solution (2.4 mg/mL) remain nearly constant before and after 3-d standing at room temperature. According to the previous study [31], the UV–vis absorption peaks of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) suspension (0.1 mg/mL) decreased from 0.9 (2 h) to 0.617 (144 h), marking a sharp decline of 31%. This indicates that CDs exhibit a superior long-term dispersity stability compared with CNTs. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 4 b, the maximum PL spectra of CDs-incorporating simulated pore solution remain almost unchanged. In brief, all the phenomena above indicate that the as-prepared CDs possess excellent long-term dispersity stability, satisfying the essential precondition for the application of CDs in enhancing the mechanical performance of cement-based materials.

To investigate the size-induced effect of CDs on mechanical behaviors of mortar, the compressive strength of mortar with 0.1 wt% L-CDs, M-CDs, or S-CDs is measured after 7-, 14- and 28-d curing. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, all the compressive strength of mortar with or without CDs significantly improve with the extension of curing time. And regardless of curing time, three kinds of CDs with different sizes improve the compressive strength of mortars. With the sizes of CDs decreasing, the compressive strength of CDs-modified mortar increases at first and then decrease (the sizes of CDs range from 1.81 to 12.53 nm) in all the curing ages. Specifically, after 7-day curing, M-CDs-M containing M-CDs with an average size of 5.86 nm exhibits the highest compressive strength of 43.85 MPa, following by S-CDs-M modified by S-CDs with the size of 1.81 nm (41.56 MPa) and L-CDs-M containing L-CDs with the size of 12.53 (40.43 MPa), improving by 15.7%, 9.4%, and 6.6% than blank group respectively. According to the results in Fig. 5 b, the increase rate of compressive strength increases firstly and then descends with the reduction of CDs sizes irrespective of curing time. Additionally, almost all the increase rates gradually decrease with the extension of curing time, illustrating that the improvement effect of CDs weakens with the development of cement hydration. These observations above confirm that CDs with various sizes possess an effective enhancement on the mechanical properties of mortar. While the relationship between the enhancement effect and the sizes of CDs is nonlinear, and within their size range of 1.81–12.53 nm, CDs with an average size of 5.86 nm exert an optimal modification effect on mechanical behaviors of mortars. To get insight into the relationship between the improvement of mechanical performance and the sizes of CDs, the phase composition and pore structure of different mortars will be comprehensively investigated via XRD, DTG, and MIP test below.

Phase composition of various mortars after 28-d curing are systematically analyzed by XRD and DTG. As illustrated in XRD pattern ( Fig. 6 a), identical characteristic peaks corresponding to Ca(OH)2 (CH, peak at 18.1°), SiO2 (peaks at 21.2° and 26.9°), unhydrated phases (namely C2S/C3S, peak at 34.6°), and the carbonization product of CaCO3 (Cc, peak at 29.5°) are detected in all the specimens with or without CDs [32–35]. This indicates that regardless of the sizes of CDs, their incorporation fails to change the phase composition. In addition, it is generally agreed that the intensity of characteristic peak is directly proportional to its content. Compared to the blank group (C-M), the intensity of the CH peak in CDs-modified specimens become stronger, and the corresponding intensity gradually increases with the sizes of CDs decreasing. Meanwhile, the intensity of the unhydrated phases (C2S/C3S) peak exhibits a slight reduction as the sizes of CDs decrease. This suggests that CDs are able to accelerate cement hydration; And the smaller the sizes of CDs, the stronger the promotion of cement hydration. However, the difference among these specimens through qualitative analysis is not distinct enough, and C–S–H gels cannot be observed in XRD patterns on account of their amorphous structure. To solve these issues above, Rietveld method is employed to quantitatively analyze the proportion of C–S–H and other phases. As shown in Fig. 6 b, compared to the C-M group, all the specimens with CDs possess the larger proportion of C–S–H and CH, and smaller proportion of C2S/C3S. Specifically, S-CDs-M modified by CDs with the smallest size exhibits the highest C–S–H gels content (75.07%), following by M-CDs-M (72.21%) and L-CDs-M (71.04%). Correspondingly, the proportion of CH gradually increases from 7.56% to 9.57%, and the content of C2S/C3S gradually decreases (from 16.96% for L-CDs-M to 6.62% for S-CDs-M) as the sizes of CDs increase. These findings above reflect that the smaller sizes of CDs can more efficiently accelerate cement hydration, thereby generating more C-S-H gels.

Besides the XRD test, DTG is executed to investigate more detail information regarding the phase composition of diverse specimens. In the DTG curves ( Fig. 7), the mass loss peaks at the range of 50–135°C, 145–190°C, 425–480°C and 600–770°C in all the specimens are associated with the decomposition of C–S–H/Ettringite (AFt), monosulfoaluminate (AFm), CH and Cc separately [36,37]. Significantly, compared to the blank group, CDs-modified groups exhibit higher contents of hydrate productions (C–S–H/AFt, AFm). And as the sizes of CDs decrease, the content of hydrate productions gradually increases. Consequently, S-CDs-M containing the smallest-sized CDs possess the strongest mass loss peaks of C–S–H/AFt and AFm. All the observations of DTG coincide with the results of XRD. To sum up, the phase composition analyses via XRD and DTG disclose that CDs can obviously accelerate the cement hydration. And the smaller the sizes of CDs, the more efficient their acceleration effect.

The previous studies have evidenced that increasing the content of C–S–H gels is beneficial to improving the compressive strength of cement mortar [38]. According to the phase composition analyses, CDs can significantly promote the hydration of cement to generate more C–S–H. And the smaller the CDs sizes, the more the production of C–S–H. Thus, the group containing the smallest size of CDs (namely, S-CDs-M) is supposed to exhibit the highest compressive strength. However, this is not the case. With the reduction of CDs sizes ranging from 1.81–12.53 nm, the compressive strength of CDs-modified mortar increases at first and then reduces. Therefore, the mere investigation of phase composition is not sufficient to understand the size-induced effect on mortar compressive strength. It is well-known that the mechanical properties of cement-based materials depend not only on the content of C–S–H gels but also on the microstructure [39,40]. Therefore, it is essential to measure the pore structure of CDs-modified mortars.

Considering that the pore structure of mortar is another main influence factor of their mechanical property, MIP test is adopted to measure the cumulative pore volume and pore sizes distribution, thereby conducing to further comprehending the relationship between the mechanical property of CDs-modified mortars and the sizes of CDs. According to the cumulative intrusion-pore diameter curves ( Fig. 8 a), the incorporation of CDs can significantly reduce the cumulative pore volume of mortar compared to blank group (0.0716 mL/g). This is attributed to the fact that CDs can accelerate the hydration of cement to generate more C-S-H gels, thus refining the pore structure of mortar [41,42]. Specifically, M-CDs-M exhibits the lowest cumulative pore volume (0.0434 mL/g), following by L-CDs-M (0.0635 mL/g) and S-CDs-M (0.0656 mL/g), suggesting that M-CDs-M possesses the most refined pore structure. This contributes to endowing M-CDs-M with higher mechanical properties. As shown in Fig. 8 b, the pore structure information can be visually analyzed. Previous studies have indicated that pores with apertures larger than 100 nm are usually considered harmful [43,44]. Obviously, compared to the C-M group, all the CDs-modified mortar possesses lower total porosity. And the M-CDs-M group exhibits the lowest total porosity, following by L-CDs-M and S-CDs-M. This further indicates that the modification of CDs effectively reduces total porosity, and M-CDs exhibit the more effective modification effect. However, the introduction of CDs increases the porosity of harmful pores (>100 nm) compared to blank group. In detail, S-CDs-M possesses the highest porosity of harmful pores, following by L-CDs-M and M-CDs-M. This is possibly assigned to the air-entraining effect of CDs. Small-sized CDs with abundant functional groups probably act as an air-entraining agent, counteracting the positive influence of CDs on the acceleration of cement hydration to a certain extent. To sum up, as the sizes of CDs decrease from 12.53 to 1.81 nm, the total porosity and harmful pores porosity of CDs-modified mortars initially decreases and then increases. M-CDs-M containing CDs with an average size of 5.86 nm exhibits the most refined pore structure under the combined effect of the acceleration of cement hydration and air entrainment.

According to the aforementioned comprehensive investigation, the size-induced effect of CDs on the mechanical properties of mortars is rationally proposed ( Fig. 9). Based on the analyses of phase composition and pore structure, CDs affect the mechanical properties of mortars primarily from two aspects: nucleation effect and air-entraining effect. With respect to the nucleation effect, the previous studies have demonstrated that nano-materials possess nucleation effect to accelerate cement hydration [45,46]. In detail, the nucleation energy of hydration products on the surface of nano-materials is lower than in the pore solution, thus making hydration products easier to grow on the surface of nano-materials [47]. This results in the acceleration of cement hydration to generate more C-S-H, refining the interface transition zone (ITZ) and pore structure, consequently contributing to improving the mechanical behaviors of mortar. In addition, CDs, as a newfangled carbon-based nanomaterial, possess excellent long-term dispersity stability in cement pore solution, thereby more effectively exerting the nucleation effect. Regarding the effect of CDs sizes, CDs with smaller sizes contain a larger quantity of particles at the same dosage, thereby leading to an increase of nucleation sites. Meanwhile, the previous works have revealed that the specific surface area is negatively correlative to the particle diameter [20], and higher specific surface area can provide more additional nucleation sites for cement hydration [41]. As a result, the smaller the sizes of CDs, the more the nucleation sites and the higher the degree of cement hydration. The smallest-sized CDs (S-CDs) are the most effective in exerting the nucleation effect to promote cement hydration, thereby generating more C-S-H gels, following by M-CDs and L-CDs. This observation is verified through the phase composition analyses via XRD and DTG. Therefore, solely from the perspective of nucleation effect, the mechanical properties of CDs-containing mortar would gradually improve with the reduction of CDs sizes, and S-CDs-M would exhibit the highest compressive strength. However, in fact, pore structure also plays a crucial role in the mechanical performance of mortar, leading to the inconsistency between the above hypothesis and the mechanical test (Fig. 5 a).

In terms of air-entraining effect, it significantly influences the pore structure of mortar. Previous studies have reported that the air entrainment reduces the compressive strength of cement [48]. Additionally, the incorporation of GO has been found to generate more air-entrapped bubbles in cement-based materials [49]. Since CDs present similar structural and physical properties to GO, CDs share the air-entraining effect [50]. This increases the harmful pores (>100 nm) (determined by Fig. 8 b), weakening the positive effects from the nucleation effect. Similarly, in a recent study, Shi’s group also found that alkanolamine simultaneously exerted effects of the acceleration of cement hydration and the air entrainment [51]. Furthermore, the particle sizes of CDs significantly affect the intensity of air-entraining effect. According to the mechanism of air-entraining agent, the efficiency of air-entraining agent enhances as the quantity of air-entraining agent molecules increases [52]. CDs and air-entraining agent molecules share a similar mechanism for generating bubbles in cement matrices. Consequently, under the same dosage of CDs, the quantity of CDs augments with the reduction of CDs sizes, thereby improving the intensity of their air-entraining effect. The negative influence of air-entraining effect leads to an increase of harmful pore porosity, consequently diminishing the compressive strength of cement mortar.

Based on the analyses above, the nucleation effect and air-entraining effect synchronously influence the mechanical properties of mortar. Firstly, the nucleation effect of CDs-containing groups is predominant compared to the air-entraining effect. Therefore, all the mechanical properties of CDs-containing groups are higher than blank group. Subsequently, the smaller the sizes of CDs, the stronger the acceleration of cement hydration, leading to more production of C-S-H gels. This contributes to refining the microstructure, thus enhancing the compressive strength of mortar. Concurrently, as the sizes of CDs decrease, the negative effect caused by air entrainment gradually strengthens, resulting in the rise of harmful pore porosity. As for M-CDs-M, the nucleation effect far outweighs the air-entraining effect of M-CDs, thus endowing M-CDs-M with the highest compressive strength. Regarding the S-CDs with the smallest size, they exhibit the strongest acceleration effect of cement hydration; But they also present the strongest air-entraining effect, dramatically offsetting the positive effect induced by hydration promotion. This confers the smallest compressive strength on S-CDs-M among CDs-containing groups. Hence, under the combined effect of nucleation and air entrainment, the mechanical properties of mortar initially improve and subsequently reduce with the reduction of CDs sizes.

This work firstly systematically investigates the size-induced effect of CDs on mechanical properties of mortars. Crucially, the related mechanism is reasonably proposed. The main conclusions are shown as follows.
(1)
L-CDs, M-CDs, and S-CDs with distinct average diameters of 12.53, 5.86 and 1.81 nm, are controllably synthesized by altering the calcination time of citric acid, respectively. The three kinds of CDs exhibit similar surface functional groups, chemical compositions, and structural features, establishing the foundation for investigating the relationship between the mechanical properties of mortar and the sizes of CDs.
(2)
The relationship between the mechanical properties of cement mortar and the sizes of CDs is revealed. The mortar containing L-CDs (40.43 MPa), M-CDs (43.85 MPa), or S-CDs (41.56 MPa) increases the compressive strength by 6.6%, 15.7% or 9.4% after 28-d curing compared with the mortar without CDs (37.9 MPa) separately. Obviously, as the sizes of CDs reduce, the compressive strength increases firstly and then decreases. In addition, it should be noted that the compressive strength of CDs-modified mortar could be further enhanced via increasing the dosages of CDs.
(3)
Based on extensive analyses of phase composition and pore structure through XRD, TG and MIP, the incorporation of CDs effectively accelerates the cement hydration, and the smaller the sizes of CDs, the more efficient their acceleration effect. Meanwhile, the total porosity of CDs-modified mortar initially decreases and then increases with the reduction of CDs sizes.
(4)
The impact of CDs with various sizes on mechanical properties of mortar is influenced by a collective influence of nucleation and air-entraining effects. CDs with smaller sizes can more significantly accelerate cement hydration to generate more C-S-H; But they gradually intensify the air-entraining effect, promoting the production of harmful pores and offsetting the advantage of hydration acceleration. The positive effect from nucleation and the negative effect from air entrainment collectively determine the mechanical properties of cement mortar.

However, the size-induced effect of CDs on the mechanical properties of mortars has only been investigated within the range of 1.81–12.58 nm. Future studies aim to broaden the range of CDs sizes significantly. In addition, for practical engineering projects, supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) are commonly utilized. Thus, it is desirable to further clarify the effect of CDs sizes on the mechanical properties of SCMs-incorporating mortars.

Yin Hu: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Changwang Yan: Methodology, Investigation. Shuang E: Software, Methodology. Dong Lu: Validation. Chuang He: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis. Huafeng Shan: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Haijie He: Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests. Wujian-Long was the co-supervisor of corresponding author（Chuang He ）during his postdoctoral study. Therefore, in order to ensure fair review of manuscript, he should be avoided when selecting reviewers. The e-mail of Wujian-Long is longwj@szu.edu.cn. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), Project of Taizhou Science and Technology Bureau (23sfa03), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS measurement.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, L. Lei, C. Shi, Effect of diethanolisopropanolamine and ethyldiisopropylamine on hydration and strength development of cement-fly ash-limestone ternary blend, Cem. Concr. Compos. 145 (2024) 105354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanical, rheological and morphological analysis of cement-based composites with a new LAS-based air entraining agent
Constr. Build. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Mendes, T.K. Moro, A.S. Figueiredo, K.Dd.C. Silva, G.C. Silva, G.J.B. Silva, R.A.F. Peixoto, Mechanical, rheological and morphological analysis of cement-based composites with a new LAS-based air entraining agent, Constr. Build. Mater. 145 (2017) 648-661.
